cards.text: A {{c1::delegating constructor}} is a constructor that calls another constructor from the same class to do the work of {{c2::initialization}}.

To delegate to another constructor, call it in the {{c1::member initializer list}}:
ClassName() {{c2:::}} {{c3::ClassName{0}}} { }

cards.question: What does this code demonstrate?
cards.code:
class Employee
{
private:
    std::string m_name;
    int m_id;
public:
    Employee(std::string name, int id)
        : m_name{name}, m_id{id} { }

    Employee()
        : Employee{"Unnamed", 0} { }
};
cards.answer: Constructor delegation. The default constructor delegates to the parameterized constructor.
cards.explanation: Instead of duplicating initialization logic, the default constructor calls the other constructor with default values.

Delegating constructors:
  • Reduce code duplication across multiple constructors
  • Centralize initialization logic in one place
  • Make code easier to maintain and modify
  • Ensure all constructors follow same initialization patterns
examples: // Without delegation: duplicate code in each constructor
// With delegation: one constructor does the work, others delegate to it

cards.front: Can you use both delegation and member initializer list in the same constructor?
cards.back: No. If a constructor delegates to another constructor, it cannot also initialize members in its own initializer list. It must choose one or the other.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Cannot mix delegation with member initialization. Must choose either delegation OR member initializer list, not both.
cards.explanation: When delegating, the delegating constructor cannot initialize any members itself - all initialization happens in the target constructor.

cards.front: Which constructor should contain the actual initialization logic when using delegation?
cards.back: The constructor with the most parameters should do the real work. Other constructors with fewer parameters delegate to it, providing default values for missing parameters.

cards.text: Best practice: Use delegating constructors to {{c1::reduce code duplication}} when you have multiple constructors that perform similar initialization.

cards.question: How would you add a default constructor using delegation?
cards.code:
class Fraction
{
public:
    Fraction(int num, int den)
        : m_num{num}, m_den{den} { }
    // Add default constructor here
private:
    int m_num, m_den;
};
cards.explanation: The default constructor delegates to the parameterized constructor, passing default values of 0 and 1.

cards.front: Can constructors form a delegation chain (A delegates to B, B delegates to C)?
cards.back: Yes, but be careful! Each constructor can delegate to one other constructor. However, avoid circular delegation (infinite loops). The chain must eventually reach a constructor that does actual initialization.
